standard_description: Use variables to represent quantities in a real-world or mathematical problem, and construct simple equations and inequalities to solve problems by reasoning about the quantities.
subject: Math

$${\frac{1}{2}(-12x+4)+5x=\frac{2}{3}(24)}$$

Task
Fishing Adventures rents small fishing boats to tourists for day-long fishing trips. Each boat can only carry 1200 pounds of people and gear for safety reasons. Assume the average weight of a person is 150 pounds. Each group will require 200 lbs of gear for the boat plus 10 lbs of gear for each person.
Create an inequality describing the restrictions on the number of people possible in a rented boat. Graph the solution set.
Several groups of people wish to rent a boat. Group 1 has 4 people. Group 2 has 5 people. Group 3 has 8 people. Which of the groups, if any, can safely rent a boat? What is the maximum number of people that may rent a boat?

Task
A water well drilling rig has dug to a height of –60 feet after one full day of continuous use.
Assuming the rig drilled at a constant rate, what was the height of the drill after 15 hours?
If the rig has been running constantly and is currently at a height of –143.6 feet, for how long has the rig been running?

Task
At the beginning of the month, Evan had \$24 in his account at the school bookstore. Use a variable to represent the unknown quantity in each transaction below and write an equation to represent it. Then represent each transaction on a number line. What is the unknown quantity in each case?
First he bought some notebooks and pens that cost \$16.
Then he deposited some more money and his account balance was \$28.
Then he bought a book for English class that cost \$34.
Then he deposited exactly enough money so that he paid off his debt to the bookstore.
Explain why it makes sense to use a negative number to represent Evan's account balance when he owes money.

khan_exercises.title: Find the mistake in one-step equations
khan_exercises.description: Practice spotting the mistake in someone else's work as they attempt to solve one-step equations.
topic_path: Find the mistake in one-step equations

items.question_content:
Carly tried to solve an equation.

$\begin{align}
7a&=28\\\\
\dfrac{7a}a&=\dfrac{28}7&\tealE{\text{Setting up}}\\\\
7&=4&\purpleD{\text{Calculating}}
\end{align}$

**Where did Carly make their first mistake?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

To $\tealE{\text{set up}}$ the equation for solving, Carly needs to perform the same operation on both sides of the equation.

To $\purpleD{\text{calculate}}$ the solution, Carly needs to correctly solve the equation they $\tealE{\text{set up}}$.

If Carly both $\tealE{\text{set up}}$ and $\purpleD{\text{calculated}}$ the solution correctly, then they correctly solved the equation.

When $\tealE{\text{setting up}}$ the equation for solving, Carly divided the left side of the equation by $a$ and the right side of the equation by $7$.

This is a mistake!

Carly should have divided both sides of the equation by $7$.

###Answer

Carly's first mistake was in $\tealE{\text{setting up}}$.

items.question_content:
Alanna tried to solve an equation.

$\begin{align}
4c&=12\\\\
\dfrac{4c}{4}&=12\cdot4&\tealE{\text{Setting up}}\\\\
c&=48&\purpleD{\text{Calculating}}
\end{align}$

**Where did Alanna make her first mistake?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
###Strategy

To $\tealE{\text{set up}}$ the equation for solving, Alanna needs to perform the same operation on both sides of the equation.

To $\purpleD{\text{calculate}}$ the solution, Alanna needs to correctly solve the equation she $\tealE{\text{set up}}$.

If Alanna both $\tealE{\text{set up}}$ and $\purpleD{\text{calculated}}$ the solution correctly, then she correctly solved the equation.

When $\tealE{\text{setting up}}$ the equation for solving, Alanna divided the left side of the equation by $4$ and multiplied the right side of the equation by $4$.

This is a mistake!

Alanna should have divided both sides of the equation by $4$.

###Answer

Alanna's first mistake was in $\tealE{\text{setting up}}$.

Trent's first mistake was in $\tealE{\text{setting up}}$.

items.question_content:
Oscar tried to solve an equation.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac34+m&=\dfrac54\\\\
\dfrac34+m-\dfrac34&=\dfrac54+\dfrac34&\tealE{\text{Setting up}}\\\\
m&=2&\purpleD{\text{Calculating}}
\end{align}$

**Where did Oscar make his first mistake?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
###Strategy

To $\tealE{\text{set up}}$ the equation for solving, Oscar needs to perform the same operation on both sides of the equation.

To $\purpleD{\text{calculate}}$ the solution, Oscar needs to correctly solve the equation he $\tealE{\text{set up}}$.

If Oscar both $\tealE{\text{set up}}$ and $\purpleD{\text{calculated}}$ the solution correctly, then he correctly solved the equation.

When $\tealE{\text{setting up}}$ the equation for solving, Oscar subtracted $\dfrac{3}{4}$ from the left side of the equation and added $\dfrac{3}{4}$ to the right side of the equation.

This is a mistake!

Oscar should have subtracted $\dfrac{3}{4}$ from both sides of the equation.

###Answer

Oscar's first mistake was in $\tealE{\text{setting up}}$.

$\begin{align}

items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

Anna wants to celebrate her birthday by eating pizza with her friends.  For $\$42.50$ total, they can buy $p$ boxes of pizza. Each box of pizza costs $\$8.50$. 

**Select the equation that matches this situation.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Anna has $\blue{$42.50}$ to pay for pizza.

She will buy an unknown number of pizzas, which we are calling $\green{p}$. 

Each pizza costs $\pink{$8.50}$.
To find the total number of pizzas that Anna can buy, we can take the amount that she has to spend and divide it by the cost of each pizza.

The following equation matches this situation:

>$\dfrac{\blue{42.5}}{\pink{8.5}}=\green{p}$

 $\blue{13} + \green{p}$

$\blue{25} + \green{m} = \pink{65}$

items.question_content:
Carly tried to solve an equation.

\end{align}$

hints:
###Strategy

To $\tealE{\text{set up}}$ the equation for solving, Carly needs to perform the same operation on both sides of the equation.

If Carly both $\tealE{\text{set up}}$ and $\purpleD{\text{calculated}}$ the solution correctly, then they correctly solved the equation.

When $\tealE{\text{setting up}}$ the equation for solving, Carly divided the left side of the equation by $a$ and the right side of the equation by $7$.

This is a mistake!

Carly should have divided both sides of the equation by $7$.

###Answer

Carly's first mistake was in $\tealE{\text{setting up}}$.
